question.text: A copper processor needs 100 MT of copper in 3 months. Current spot: Rs 750/kg, 3-month futures: Rs 770/kg. The processor's convenience yield for holding physical copper is Rs 30/kg. Should the processor buy spot or futures?

answer.text: Buy in spot market — effective futures cost (Rs 770) exceeds spot + carry - convenience yield
Buy futures at Rs 770 as it is always cheaper than spot
Wait for prices to fall further before buying
Buy half in spot and half in futures for diversification

explanation.text: **The Logic:** With convenience yield of Rs 30/kg, the processor values spot ownership at Rs 750 + carry - 30. If carry (financing + storage) for 3 months is less than Rs 50 (770-750+30=50), buying spot is advantageous because the processor gets the convenience yield benefit of uninterrupted production. **The Trap:** Futures are not always cheaper — convenience yield makes spot holding more attractive for actual users/processors. Simple price comparison ignores the production benefit. **Cross-Exam Context:** Convenience yield application is unique to commodity markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Industrial processors should evaluate the full cost including convenience yield — for them, stock-outs (running out of raw material) can be far more costly than the carrying charges.
